summary_opm:
TECHNICAL AND MANAGERIAL EVALUATION COMMENT

The evaluation results reflect a solid upward trajectory in the engineer’s technical and managerial performance. With functional area scores ranging from 47.50% to 60.00%, the engineer demonstrates growing capability in planning, coordination, leadership, and execution. The consistently improving ratings across multiple categories indicate stronger ownership of responsibilities and a more structured approach to project delivery.

Across the evaluation period, several strengths were clearly observed:

  • Operational planning shows improved foresight, with work plans becoming more structured, aligned with milestones, and better communicated to crews and subcontractors.

  • Risk management practices are becoming more proactive, with better anticipation of threats to completed works and more consistent implementation of preventive controls.

  • Project execution reflects stronger schedule alignment, more accurate progress tracking, and improved responsiveness to delays through recovery planning.

  • Stakeholder coordination has strengthened, particularly in communication, documentation, and timely submission of reports and inspection records.

  • Team leadership shows notable growth, with clearer direction to foremen, better handling of pressure, and stronger presence on site.

  • Subcontractor management demonstrates firmer enforcement of expectations and improved alignment across trades.

  • Process improvement efforts show increasing use of digital tools, better documentation of lessons learned, and early steps toward workflow optimization.

While performance is trending positively, several areas still require continued focus:

  • Ensuring full and consistent understanding of work packages and method statements across all crews.

  • Further strengthening documentation accuracy, traceability, and audit readiness.

  • Enhancing schedule logic, critical path awareness, and integration of reporting tools.

  • Deepening leadership consistency, particularly in coaching, delegation, and accountability.

  • Expanding the use of digital tools for QA/QC, progress tracking, and variation management.

Overall, the engineer demonstrates meaningful progress and a growing level of maturity in managing technical and operational responsibilities. The combination of improved planning discipline, stronger coordination, and increasing leadership presence indicates readiness for greater responsibility as competencies continue to develop. With sustained focus on documentation, schedule governance, and process optimization, the engineer is expected to deliver even stronger performance in the upcoming evaluation cycle.
summary_qaqc:
QAQC PERFORMANCE EVALUATION COMMENT
Erwyn has shown progressive improvement in his QAQC performance over the four‑month period, steadily increasing his score from 44% in November to 51% in February. While his initial results were below the Project Manager threshold, this is understandable given the newness of the QAQC scoring system and the impact of site non‑conformances for which he is accountable. His consistent upward trend demonstrates growing alignment with QAQC expectations and reflects his proven ability to adapt, as seen in his previous project performance. With continued focus on preventive controls, documentation, and non‑conformance management, Erwyn is well‑positioned to further elevate his performance in the next evaluation cycle.

Functional Area Highlights

Quality Governance — 50.50%

Shows improved oversight, stronger coordination with QAQC, and better enforcement of quality protocols.

QAQC Planning & Control — 48.50%

Demonstrates progress in integrating QAQC milestones into project sequencing and proactively addressing issues.

Technical Documentation Oversight — 35.00%

This remains the lowest‑scoring area and requires significant improvement, particularly in documentation completeness, traceability, and digital archiving.

Field Decision‑Making & Issue Resolution — 54.50%

One of his strongest areas, showing maturity in handling non‑conformances and applying sound engineering judgment.


Performance Summary From November to February, Erwyn Macusi recorded QAQC monthly averages of 44%, 46%, 48%, and 51%, showing a steady and consistent upward trend. Based on the company’s threshold ranges, his February score places him within the Project Manager 1 band (50.1%–58%), indicating that he is beginning to align with the expected performance level for his position.

Contextual Factors

  • New QAQC System: This evaluation cycle uses a newly implemented QAQC scoring system. As expected during its initial rollout, scores across multiple sites—including Erwyn’s—are generally lower due to unfamiliarity with the new scoring mechanics, documentation requirements, and evaluation criteria.

  • Impact of Non‑Conformances: Erwyn’s performance is significantly affected by the non‑conformance records documented on his site. Since he is accountable for site compliance, these NCs directly influence his monthly percentages. The presence of NCs explains the lower starting scores and highlights areas requiring stronger preventive controls.

  • Positive Upward Movement: Despite these challenges, Erwyn demonstrated continuous improvement month after month. This upward trajectory reflects increasing familiarity with the system, better documentation practices, and more effective QAQC oversight.

  • Adaptability Based on Previous Projects: Erwyn’s strong performance in his previous assignment shows that he adapts quickly to new environments and systems. His current improvement trend is consistent with this history and indicates that he is capable of reaching higher QAQC performance levels with continued focus.

RECOMMENDATIONS FOR IMPROVEMENT

  • 1. Strengthen Preventive QAQC Controls

    • Reduce the occurrence of non‑conformances through stricter pre‑work inspections and improved subcontractor oversight.

    • Reinforce discipline in following ITPs, method statements, and sequencing logic.

    2. Improve Technical Documentation Oversight

    • Enhance the completeness, accuracy, and traceability of QAQC documentation.

    • Ensure NCRs, CAPAs, and test logs are properly recorded and archived.

    3. Organize and Maintain Electronic QAQC Documents

    • Begin systematically organizing all QAQC‑related electronic documents for easy access during monthly performance evaluations.

    • This supports the company’s digital transformation initiative and reinforces the accountability of the Project Head in maintaining complete, accessible, and well‑structured digital records.

    4. Strengthen Coordination with QAQC Personnel

    • Improve communication and scheduling of inspections to avoid delays and ensure timely closure of issues.

    5. Implement a Corrective Action Tracker

    • Maintain a structured log for all NCs, including root cause analysis, assigned owners, deadlines, and closure verification.

    6. Leverage His Proven Adaptability

    • Provide structured guidance on the new QAQC system.

    • Assign targeted coaching to accelerate his transition into updated processes.

  • Organize and Maintain Electronic Documents

    • Begin systematically organizing all QS‑related electronic documents for easy access during monthly performance evaluations.

    • This supports the company’s digital transformation initiative and reinforces the accountability of the Project Head in maintaining complete, accessible, and well‑structured digital records.to be Included on demerits log.
summary_qs:
QS PERFORMANCE EVALUATION COMMENT

Based on the QS evaluation results from November to February, Erwyn Macusi consistently performed within the Project Manager 1 threshold (50.1%–58%), with monthly averages of 54%, 54%, 56%, and 56%. This reflects a stable and slightly improving performance trend, showing that he is maintaining the expected competency level for his position.

His strongest functional area is Measurement & Cost Estimation, where he achieved an average of 69%, placing him within the Project Manager 3 band (67.1%–75%). This demonstrates his strong technical capability, accuracy in quantity take‑offs, and ability to validate field data with consistency. His performance in this area also reflects his capacity to produce detailed estimates and complete cost‑proposal packages, a strength he has consistently shown in his previous project assignments.

However, his score of 41% in Cost Control & Value Management indicates a key area for improvement. This falls within the Project Superintendent 2 range (33.1%–42%), suggesting the need to strengthen budget monitoring, variation order preparation, and value‑engineering initiatives. This lower score is understandable given that the quantity take‑off and estimation system is not yet fully established, and the current procedures are still under development. The absence of a standardized system affects consistency and efficiency, which naturally impacts his scoring in this category.

Despite these challenges, Erwyn has a proven history of quick adaptation. His performance in previous projects shows that once a system is properly established and structured, he can easily adjust and deliver high‑quality outputs. With the ongoing development of the QS measurement and estimation procedures, Erwyn is expected to adapt rapidly and elevate his performance further once the system becomes fully operational.

RECOMMENDATIONS FOR IMPROVEMENT
1. Strengthen Cost Control Discipline Improve monitoring of expenditures against approved budgets. -Establish a more structured system for tracking cost variances and forecasting impacts. 2. Enhance VO Documentation and Justification -Ensure all variation order requests are supported by complete, traceable, and timely documentation. Coordinate closely with site teams to validate field conditions before VO preparation. 3. Develop Value‑Engineering Mindset -Proactively identify cost‑efficient alternatives that do not compromise quality. -Engage earlier in planning discussions to influence design and procurement decisions. 4. Deepen Familiarity With the New QS Framework -Allocate time to fully internalize the updated QS scoring system and documentation pathways. -Integrate QS checkpoints into weekly planning and reporting routines. 5. Improve Cross‑Functional Coordination -Strengthen collaboration with QAQC, Engineering, and Procurement to ensure alignment between quantities, cost impacts, and site execution.
